caveats: a: OEWS is four-digit NAICS 326100, not six-digit 326130.
a: Task shares are inferred, not directly measured.
a: Processes range from standardized continuous sheet to labor-intensive batch laminates, creating large plant-level variation.
rho: No five-year adoption study specific to laminated plastics was found.
rho: The same vision system may perform differently across textures, colors, reinforcements, and low-volume custom runs.

e: Public descriptions often blur lamination, stock-shape distribution, machining, and downstream fabrication.

s5: Strategic acquisitions reported publicly skew toward larger engineered-material companies and may not represent LMM firms.

q: Retention likely differs markedly between commodity sheet and engineered electrical, aerospace, defense, or industrial laminates.
d5: Both demand sources are broader than 326130.
d5: Value-based end-use shares do not directly measure constant-quality laminate volume.
d5: Environmental restrictions on resin systems and shifts in imported stock could change domestic demand.
o: No direct operator-requirement study exists.
o: The estimate separates elimination of the current service basket from labor reduction within the manufacturing operator.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

identity.title: Laminated Plastics Plate, Sheet (except Packaging), and Shape Manufacturing

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat B2B sales of standard and customer-specified laminated stock under purchase orders, blanket agreements, distributor programs, and qualified supply relationships, priced by sheet, unit, area, weight, or production run.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive lamination operations, flexible packaging laminators, unlaminated sheet and profile makers, downstream fabricators with no lamination manufacturing, shell entities, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market manufacturers repeatedly supplying laminated plastic plate, nonpackaging sheet, rod, and other shapes made by bonding or resin-impregnating profiles and compressing them under heat.

a.bridge: BLS measures occupations and wages for all NAICS 326100 plastics-product manufacturing rather than 326130 or task exposure. I map portions of quoting, specification review, purchasing, production planning, quality inspection, press/line monitoring, maintenance, packaging, material movement, and administration to AI capability and weight toward the production payroll. Laminating may involve more batch records, visual inspection, and specification work than broad commodity molding, so the center is modestly above a highly automated continuous-process case; this could overstate exposure where presses and inspection are already automated.
caveats: OEWS is four-digit NAICS 326100, not six-digit 326130.
Task shares are inferred, not directly measured.
Processes range from standardized continuous sheet to labor-intensive batch laminates, creating large plant-level variation.
a.change_evidence: A 326130 wage-weighted task census across thermoset and thermoplastic laminate plants, distinguishing already-automated controls from manual layup, press operation, trimming, inspection, lab testing, maintenance, planning, quoting, and administrative tasks.
a.rationale: The broad BLS mix is production-heavy: metal and plastic workers, assemblers, other production workers, packers, and material movers dominate relative to management and business occupations. AI can assist visual defect detection, cure and process monitoring, maintenance prediction, scheduling, technical-document review, quoting, and office work. Layup, material staging, press loading, finishing, testing, packing, and maintenance remain substantially physical.

d5.bridge: BLS projects all NAICS 326100 real output from $190.7 billion in 2024 to $212.4 billion in 2034, or 1.1% annually; applying that rate for five years gives about 1.056. The plastics-industry report finds real shipments grew only 0.2% annually in 2014-2024 and shows broad end-use diversification, while 326130 may be more exposed to cyclical durable goods and capital projects. I center slightly below the BLS five-year implication and widen the range; broad data may overstate growth if laminated stock loses share or understate it if engineered composites gain share.
caveats: Both demand sources are broader than 326130.
Value-based end-use shares do not directly measure constant-quality laminate volume.
Environmental restrictions on resin systems and shifts in imported stock could change domestic demand.
d5.change_evidence: U.S. five-year shipment and volume data for laminated plastic stock by resin, reinforcement, and end market from Census product tables, CompositesWorld or industry vendors, distributor sell-through, and customer production forecasts.
d5.rationale: Laminated plate and sheet serve a diversified set of industrial, construction, transportation, electrical, and service-related uses. Broad plastics demand has been resilient and BLS expects modest real-output growth, but laminates compete with metals, wood products, glass, unlaminated plastics, and newer composite or additive solutions. Capital spending and durable-goods cycles can produce volatility.

caveats: Eligibility is not directly measured and the frozen count is margin-bridged.
Public descriptions often blur lamination, stock-shape distribution, machining, and downstream fabrication.
e.change_evidence: Firm-level review of the 76 frozen candidates verifying primary process, packaging exclusion, independent ownership, external revenue, repeat-order share, customer qualification, normalized EBITDA, and transferable equipment and permits.
e.rationale: Independent laminators typically sell repeated physical output to external industrial customers and fit the outsourced-supply lens. Exclusions include captive operations, firms dominated by one-off development or downstream fabrication, misclassified packaging businesses, distressed plants without transferable customer qualifications, and businesses whose economics do not support normalized LMM EBITDA.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 326130 ($1,477,255,006) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 326130 ($4,489,936,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1465 (margins.json[32]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Paper/Forest Products EBITDA/Sales 14.65% (mid-range for wood/paper/plastics)); revenue/employee $375,758 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 326130); class <5: 51 firms, avg revenue/firm $611,843 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $89,635 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 32 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,238,906 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $181,500 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 19 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,192,632 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $467,721 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 49 firms, avg revenue/firm $12,163,816 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,781,999 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 27 firms, avg revenue/firm $53,384,889 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $7,820,886 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 27 firms, avg revenue/firm $85,963,259 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $12,593,617 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 76

caveats: No direct operator-requirement study exists.
The estimate separates elimination of the current service basket from labor reduction within the manufacturing operator.
o.change_evidence: Customer and distributor interviews on insourcing, imports, additive manufacturing, near-net-shape composites, and substitution by metal, glass, wood composite, or unlaminated polymers over the next five years.
o.rationale: The current basket consists of physical engineered stock requiring accountable material selection, bonding or impregnation, heat and pressure processing, testing, traceability, and delivery. Software can optimize these activities but cannot replace the product or responsible manufacturer. Remaining displacement risk comes from customer insourcing, imports, material substitution, or near-net-shape manufacturing that bypasses laminated stock.

caveats: Contract terms and product differentiation were not observed.
Retention likely differs markedly between commodity sheet and engineered electrical, aerospace, defense, or industrial laminates.
q.change_evidence: A representative contract and invoice sample measuring fixed-price duration, resin and reinforcement pass-through, annual cost-down clauses, distributor rebates, rebid cadence, customer concentration, qualification switching cost, and margins following process improvements.
q.rationale: Custom formulations, certifications, surface requirements, tooling, and qualification can create switching costs and permit an operator to retain savings during contract periods. Retention falls where laminated stock is standardized, distributor-priced, resin-indexed, or subject to annual customer cost-downs and competitive rebids.

caveats: No five-year adoption study specific to laminated plastics was found.
The same vision system may perform differently across textures, colors, reinforcements, and low-volume custom runs.
rho.change_evidence: Plant-level deployment data showing sensor and MES coverage, AI pilot conversion, labor hours saved, defect escape and false-reject rates, downtime effects, integration cost, and customer requalification requirements.
rho.rationale: Vision inspection, predictive maintenance, production scheduling, document extraction, and quote assistance are feasible over five years. Implementation is restrained by small-firm capital and engineering capacity, legacy presses and controls, limited labeled defect data, changing colors and surface finishes, customer qualification, destructive-test requirements, cybersecurity, and the need for accountable quality release.

caveats: No six-digit deal-rate or owner-age series was found.
Strategic acquisitions reported publicly skew toward larger engineered-material companies and may not represent LMM firms.
s5.change_evidence: PitchBook, CapIQ, GF Data, Axial, and specialist composites/plastics broker transaction records for U.S. laminate manufacturers from 2016-2025, matched to eligible firms, plus owner-age and succession-plan interviews.
s5.rationale: A low-twenties five-year transfer rate allows for private-company succession and strategic consolidation while excluding family/internal succession, minority recapitalizations, indefinite holds, asset closures, and sale processes that fail or do not transfer an operating firm.

principal_driver: Specification-heavy repeat production creates AI opportunities in quality, process control, quoting, and documentation.
principal_weakness: Six-digit demand and occupation evidence is thin, and much of the high labor base remains tied to physical production.
